question.text: When does a put option buyer choose to exercise the option?

answer.text: When the underlying price is below the strike price
When the underlying price is above the strike price
When the underlying price equals the strike price
When the premium increases above the strike price

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A put buyer has the right to sell at the strike price. This is profitable when the market price is below the strike, allowing the buyer to sell at a higher-than-market price. **The Trap:** Underlying above strike is when a CALL buyer exercises. At the money, there is no gain from exercise. **Cross-Exam Context:** Exercise conditions for puts and calls are fundamental concepts tested in all NISM derivative modules. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Put buyer benefits from falling prices. Think of it as insurance: you exercise your fire insurance only when there is a fire (price decline).
